The engineer said the root cause was a bad config.
It might be worth rolling back, rather than shipping tonight.
If I were you, I would escalate it now.
Ana, welcome to your midpoint review. No pressure -- just tell me, how do you feel about your English now?
Honestly, much more confident. Six months ago, I would have frozen on a call like this.
Give me one real example. Where did your English make a difference?
Last week, during an outage, I reported what the on-call engineer had said and flagged a discrepancy in the logs.
Nice. And if you had not caught that discrepancy?
If we had not caught it, the root cause would have stayed hidden, and the outage would have been worse.
Good. Now, a hard one -- would you ever push back on a manager?
I would. I would signpost my answer, then say it might be worth rolling back, rather than shipping, if I were unsure.
That is exactly the benchmark I wanted. Let me reiterate your goal for the second half: keep speaking, and be a little bolder.
If we had caught it sooner, the outage would have been shorter.
She said the root cause was a bad config.
Let me signpost my answer: there are two parts to this.
I will follow up on that and get back to you.

So, you have reached the midpoint. Half of the program is behind you, and this is a good moment to stop and take stock. A midpoint review is not a test, and it is not new material; it is a benchmark. We look back at how far your English has come, and we set the target for the second half. Think about where you started. In the early lessons, you were still building the basic words, requirement, bug, deadline, deploy. Now those words are automatic, and you have added much harder ones, root cause, trade-off, to mitigate, to escalate. But the real change is not the vocabulary; it is the structures. You can now say what would have happened if a test had failed. You can report what a client said on a call, shifting the tenses back. You can soften a hard recommendation so it does not sound blunt. Here is my honest assessment, and I want you to hear it clearly. The single biggest jump has been your fluency. Six months ago, when a question went off-script, you froze. Now you hold the floor, you signpost your answer, and you keep talking until the idea lands. That is the benchmark that matters. So for the second half, the goal is not more words; it is confidence. Keep speaking, keep making mistakes out loud, and keep going. You are exactly where you should be.

Hey Ana, it's Karen, your team lead. I just saw that you hit the midpoint of your English program, and I wanted to leave you a quick note, because honestly, the difference on our calls is huge. I remember your first stand-ups, when you would prepare every sentence in advance and read it. This week, in the incident review, you spoke completely off the cuff. You reported what the on-call engineer had said, you flagged the discrepancy in the logs, and when I pushed back, you did not freeze; you signposted your answer and walked me through it in two clear parts. That is a different person from six months ago. So here is my one piece of feedback for the second half. You are clear and you are accurate; now I want you to be a little bolder. Do not soften everything. When you are sure the root cause is a bad config, say so directly, and only hedge when you genuinely are not sure. Trust your English; it is good enough now to carry a strong opinion. I am really proud of you, and I will follow up after your next review so we can set the goals together. Keep it up, Ana.

Here is how Ana prepared for her midpoint review with her mentor. First, she looked back through her notes and collected the key words she had learned, from requirement and root cause to trade-off and outage. Then, she picked one real story for each structure: a conditional, a piece of reported speech, and a diplomatic recommendation. After that, she practiced telling each story out loud, without a script, until it felt natural. Next, she wrote down the two areas she still wanted to improve in the second half. Finally, she walked into the benchmark call calmly, ready to show how far her English had come.
